main_summary.summary: Freeman A. Hrabowski III, president of the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC), recounts his Birmingham childhood, his role as a twelve-year-old child marcher in the May 1963 Children's Crusade, and his subsequent career building Black STEM achievement through the Meyerhoff Scholars Program. Born August 1950 in the Titusville middle-class neighborhood of Birmingham, Hrabowski describes parents who taught in Birmingham city schools and at the steel mill, a Sixth Avenue Baptist Church pastored by Reverend John T. Porter and previously Reverend John Goodgame, and a school community that produced Condoleezza Rice (his counselor's daughter) and Alma Vivian Johnson Powell (his principal's niece). He recounts hearing Martin Luther King Jr. propose involving children in the Birmingham campaign, his parents' overnight deliberation before allowing him to march, being spat upon and arrested at the city hall steps, five days in juvenile detention, and the September 1963 16th Street Baptist Church bombing that killed his classmate Cynthia Wesley and Denise McNair. He traces his path from Ullman High School through Hampton Institute to a doctorate from the University of Illinois, and his 1989 founding with philanthropist Robert E. Meyerhoff of the UMBC scholars program that has produced hundreds of Black PhDs and MD-PhDs.
key_themes: Birmingham Children's Crusade and child arrest
16th Street Baptist Church bombing and funeral
Black middle-class Birmingham church and school community
Meyerhoff Scholars Program and Black STEM pipeline
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hrabowski is one of the youngest documented leaders of the May 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade — at twelve he led a group of younger children into arrest at the city hall steps and spent five days in juvenile detention, where Dr. King addressed parents and incarcerated children through the bars. His first-person account names Eugene 'Bull' Connor, Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth, attorney Arthur Shores, Judge (likely Hobart Grooms or Frank Johnson Jr.) who ordered the suspended Children's Crusade students reinstated, and the school principal George C. Bell who conducted the suspension assembly as a university induction. His recounting of attending the funeral for Cynthia Wesley, Denise McNair, and the other 16th Street Baptist Church victims, and seeing rabbis, Catholic priests, and Baptist ministers at the service, is a primary source on interfaith solidarity at that moment.

The interview also documents the Birmingham Black middle-class network — Titusville and Smithfield (the latter nicknamed 'Dynamite Hill' for recurring KKK bombings), Sixth Avenue Baptist Church as sister to 16th Street Baptist, the Birmingham World's reporting that more Negroes owned homes in Birmingham than any U.S. city — and connects that community to subsequent American leadership through Condoleezza Rice (daughter of Hrabowski's high-school counselor Reverend John W. Rice) and Alma Vivian Johnson Powell (niece of his principal George C. Bell). Hrabowski's 1989 founding of the Meyerhoff Scholars Program at UMBC with Robert E. Meyerhoff is documented as a direct extension of the Children's Crusade lesson that systemic conditions can be changed.

chapters.title: Birmingham Family and Community
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes his August 1950 birth into a Titusville middle-class household: parents born 1910 and 1916, married 1940, both educated through Alabama State University in Montgomery. His father came from Lowndes County, Alabama, near Selma; his mother from Tuskegee. The family worshipped at Sixth Avenue Baptist Church, sister church to the one later bombed. Hrabowski's father worked at the steel mill while doing literate work for white supervisors who lacked a high-school education.
related_events: Sixth Avenue Baptist Church relocation under UAB expansion

chapters.title: Black Middle-Class Birmingham
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes the rhythms of middle-class Black Birmingham — being constantly in church, Reverend John Goodgame followed by Reverend John T. Porter, youth fellowship, the Birmingham World's reporting that more Negroes owned homes in Birmingham than any U.S. city, and the segregated retail and entertainment system (Kiddie Land, balcony movie seating, restaurants with side doors). He recalls discovering the second-hand textbooks from the white school and the teacher's instruction that 'you don't have time to be a victim.'

chapters.title: Parents as Race People
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes his parents as 'race people' — his mother's late-1940s firing for organizing teachers to demand equalization of teacher salaries before being hired by Dr. C.R. Hayes, the director of Colored Schools in Birmingham; his father's work writing the Negro section of the steel-mill bulletin and tutoring men for their GEDs. The family belonged to the NAACP and the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights. He recalls his early reading, being hidden in the girls' restroom during VIP visits because his mother had concealed her pregnancy to keep teaching.
keywords: Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights

chapters.title: Awareness of the System
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes growing awareness of police as unfair, his father being addressed as 'Mr.' until officers noticed Hrabowski's hair and reverted to 'boy.' He recounts the climate of fear after bombings of attorney Arthur Shores and the Shuttlesworths in Smithfield, the proximity of victims to his church community, and the discussions in youth fellowship led by Reverend John W. Rice — Condoleezza Rice's father — about critical thinking and American democracy.

chapters.title: First Hearing Dr. King
chapters.summary: Hrabowski, attending church meetings he did not want to attend, first heard Martin Luther King Jr. propose involving children in the Birmingham march so children of color could attend the best schools in the city. He recounts asking his parents' permission, being refused, calling them hypocrites, and the next-morning reversal after they prayed all night. King's message — that the world did not have to be the way it was — broke the prior understanding that injustice was simply the way of the world.

chapters.title: Marching and Arrest
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes training meetings at Sixth Avenue Baptist Church, the freedom songs ('Ain't going to let nobody turn me around,' 'Keep on walking, keep on talking, watching onto Freedom Land'), kneeling to pray at the city hall steps, and being spat upon and arrested by a red-faced officer. As a child over fourteen — actually twelve and a half but in a higher grade — he led a group of younger children. Teachers had been told that if their children went to jail, the teachers would lose their jobs; many middle-class families had also been told their home mortgages were at risk.

chapters.title: Five Days in Juvenile Detention
chapters.summary: Hrabowski recounts five days in juvenile detention, kept from his parents, sharing space with 'bad boys' encouraged to harass the marchers. He read aloud from Psalm 23 to deflect threats. Through the bars he heard Dr. King address the gathered parents, telling them their actions would determine the future of children to come. The detention experience taught him that children can make decisions that shape their lives and know the difference between right and wrong.
related_events: May 1963 mass arrests of child marchers

chapters.title: Suspension and Federal Court Order
chapters.summary: Released from detention, Hrabowski was suspended from Ullman High School. Principal George C. Bell — uncle of Alma Vivian Johnson Powell and the figure Hrabowski credits with making him a mathematician — conducted the suspension assembly as a university induction, framing it as a badge of courage. Teachers quietly arranged homework delivery. A federal court order (Hrabowski recalls the judge's name uncertainly as Judge Black) reinstated the suspended students; the news arrived during church and produced collective weeping at the recognition that there was decency among whites in power.
related_events: Federal court order reinstating Birmingham child marchers

chapters.title: The September 1963 Bombing
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes the September 15, 1963 bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church, sister church to his own, learned when his pastor received a note at the pulpit and stopped preaching. Cynthia Wesley, his classmate who had said 'bye, Franklin' the previous Friday, was among the four girls killed; her hand was identified by her father's ring. He attended the funeral, where Principal Bell retied his tie because it was not dark enough, and saw three small coffins including eleven-year-old Denise McNair. King declined to assign blame and acknowledged that 'life is as hard as steel.' For the first time Hrabowski saw white rabbis, Catholic priests, and Baptist ministers at a Black service.

chapters.title: Mother's Lesson on Bull Connor
chapters.summary: Hrabowski reflects on his parents' teaching that hatred eats from within — hate the act, not the person — and recalls his mother weeping when Eugene 'Bull' Connor died, telling him 'he was somebody's child' and a product of his environment. He frames his work with UMBC students from 150 countries as an outgrowth of King's teaching that character can transcend race, religion, and culture.

chapters.title: Massachusetts Summer and Hampton Institute
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes his thirteenth summer in Massachusetts in an all-white chemistry and math class, where teachers refused to call on him — what he calls his Ralph Ellison Invisible Man experience. He left Birmingham in 1966 for Hampton Institute (where Booker T. Washington had graduated before founding Tuskegee), finding decent white math and science faculty and recognizing that segregation had under-prepared both Black and white Alabama students. He met his future wife in calculus, who outperformed him on tests, and confirmed when he later took a University of Alabama at Birmingham calculus course that the white Alabama students were also poorly prepared.

chapters.title: Watching Birmingham Change
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes returning periodically to Birmingham and watching the city change — friends moving to predominantly white universities, Richard Arrington Jr.'s election as the first Black mayor (a PhD scientist), the University of Alabama at Birmingham becoming the city's biggest employer, and the simultaneous emergence of Black-on-black urban violence that he had assumed was a Northern phenomenon. His mother had refused to let him integrate Ramsay or Phillips High School, distrusting teachers who did not want Black students present.

chapters.title: Meyerhoff Scholars Program
chapters.summary: Hrabowski describes his University of Illinois graduate-school encounter with the topology professor who wrote 'you did surprisingly well' on his A-minus test, the realization that no predominantly white university was educating ten Black STEM PhDs a year, and the 1989 founding of the Meyerhoff Scholars Program with Jewish philanthropist Robert E. Meyerhoff. The program has produced hundreds of Black PhDs and MD-PhDs now on the medical faculties of Harvard, Stanford, Duke, Johns Hopkins, and Michigan. He chairs a National Academies committee on scaling these numbers further.

chapters.title: What the Movement Was About
chapters.summary: Hrabowski closes by noting that in 1963 only about two percent of African Americans had completed college, while the figure is now near twenty percent. The civil rights movement was about opportunity, but opportunity alone is not enough: families and communities must focus on education, values, and the connection between hard work and dreams. He extends the concern to poor white children, arguing that race still matters but the underclass challenge crosses racial lines.

loc_video.caption: Freeman A. Hrabowski oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Baltimore, Maryland, 2011 August 14
